commands.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

commands.author: heichaowo

target.title: fix(telegram): skip mirror write when primary reply already exists (#94930)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94930.

Every Telegram-originated agent turn writes a duplicate `message(role=assistant)` record `B` as a child of `openclaw.cache-ttl` ~1s after the primary reply `A`. `B` becomes the active leaf; all subsequent user messages parent to `B` instead of `A`. `A` becomes an orphaned branch, causing:

- Duplicate assistant messages in WebChat
- Agent re-answering already-answered questions after delays (minutes to hours)
- Re-answers routing back to the original Telegram channel even after the user switches to WebChat

**Observed on v2026.6.8 — 100% reproducible from Telegram, not reproducible from WebChat native.**

## Root cause

Two writers touch the session transcript per Telegram turn:

- **A**: embedded runner (normal agent execution path — `appendAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`)
- **B**: `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage()` **directly**, bypassing the facade and its delivery-mirror dedup helpers

After prompt+compaction, `attempt.ts` appends an `openclaw.cache-ttl` custom node which becomes the active leaf. ~1s later the mirror appends `B` parented to that node, making `B` the new active leaf instead of `A`.

`appendSessionTranscriptMessageLocked` has an idempotency check via `readMessageIdempotencyKey`, but the mirror writer's constructed message has **no `idempotencyKey`** field — the check returns `undefined` and is skipped, so `B` is written unconditionally.

**Note:** commit `f5eddc2b6d` patched `findLatestEquivalentAssistantMessageId` in `transcript.ts` and the WebChat rendering layer, but the Telegram mirror writer calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage` directly — that facade path patch does not prevent the duplicate write.

## Fix

In `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` (`extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`), before calling `appendSessionTranscriptMessage`, read the transcript tail via `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` and compare its text to the mirror text. If they match, skip the mirror write.

`readTail` (unlike the previous `readLatest`) stops scanning at the first user-role line it encounters, so the comparison is strictly bounded to the **current turn**. This closes the false-negative that `readLatest` had: after a "say it again" turn that produces the same text as a prior turn, `readLatest` would match the *previous* turn's identical reply and wrongly skip a legitimate mirror write — losing a transcript row. `readTail` cannot cross a user line, so it only matches evidence from the turn that just completed.

This approach also preserves the existing dedup behavior for the `message_tool`-only path (where the primary runner writes nothing): in that case `readTail` returns `null` and the mirror write proceeds normally.

Changes (4 files, +70/−5):
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — swap reader + concise 3-line invariant comment
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.runtime.ts` + `src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts` — re-export `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` as a named export on the existing subpath (`pnpm build`'s `check-plugin-sdk-exports` passed)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` — added regression test (prior identical assistant → user line → new mirror ⇒ writes, not skipped), plus tail-reader mock wiring

## Testing

`pnpm test extensions/telegram` → **2162 passed, 0 failed**

## Real behavior proof

### Issue addressed

Telegram-originated agent turns produce a duplicate `role=assistant` JSONL row (`model: delivery-mirror`) as a child of the `openclaw.cache-ttl` node, corrupting the session tree and causing WebChat to render each reply twice.

### Environment tested

Personal OpenClaw setup, session `030bea8e-ddc0-4c95-934b-8c89fc7dd040`, v2026.6.8, macOS, Telegram bot channel → WebChat cross-channel.

### Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Send a message to the OpenClaw agent via Telegram bot
2. Open the same session in WebChat
3. Observe that only one assistant bubble appears (no duplicate)
4. Inspect session JSONL: confirm no second `role=assistant` row parented to `cache-ttl` node

### Evidence after fix

Before fix — live JSONL from session `030bea8e` (reproduced prior to this patch):

```
14:25:09 message id=95dbc41a par=74d45537 role=assistant ← A (primary reply, embedded runner)
14:25:09 custom id=bfa1edcb par=95dbc41a kind=cache-ttl ← cache-ttl inserted as active leaf
14:25:10 message id=8d1b4c19 par=bfa1edcb role=assistant ← B (delivery-mirror duplicate)
14:26:33 message id=c3f2a801 par=8d1b4c19 role=user ← next user msg parents to B, not A
```

Full evidence with tree diagrams: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94930#issuecomment-4752524069

After fix — live JSONL from the same session with patch applied to the installed dist, Telegram-originated turn (message: "测试"), 2026-06-20T03:12 GMT+8:

```
custom id=2aaf2f5f par=918f1b2d customType=openclaw.cache-ttl
message id=3d4659ae par=2aaf2f5f role=user
message id=816e1778 par=3d4659ae role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6 ← A (primary, no B follows)
message id=f571e312 par=816e1778 role=toolResult
message id=debd14fc par=f571e312 role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6
```

No `model=delivery-mirror` row appears after the `cache-ttl` node. `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript` read the transcript tail, found the primary reply text matched, and returned early without writing.

### Observed result

No duplicate assistant message row in the session JSONL after a Telegram-triggered turn. WebChat renders one bubble per turn. Session tree corruption eliminated.

commands.author: RomneyDa

target.title: feat(qa): crabline channel driver
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes QA profile channel drivers executable and separates channel identity from transport mode.

- Adds the merged `crabline` package as a QA Lab dependency pinned to `openclaw/crabline@93b4de9b933b2c32f16f265725a0af3c429997e2`.
- Runs the `smoke-ci` QA profile through the Crabline runtime via `import("crabline")`, with `channel: telegram` and other requested channel IDs resolved through Crabline instead of an inlined local fallback.
- Adds a separate `QA Smoke CI` PR workflow for the canonical mock-channel smoke profile.
- Keeps `.github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml` focused on scheduled/manual live transport coverage.
- Makes the `release` live profile fan out to the native Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Slack, and WhatsApp live lane catalogs, then merges their `qa-evidence.json` outputs into one profile evidence artifact.

## Why

Tests should be able to ask for a channel shape such as `telegram` while the selected profile decides whether that exchange is handled by Crabline or by a real live transport. `smoke-ci` is the PR CI mock-channel profile. Live service coverage stays in the live transport lanes and release profile evidence.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check .github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml .github/workflows/qa-smoke-ci.yml docs/concepts/qa-e2e-automation.md extensions/qa-lab/package.json pnpm-workspace.yaml extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsgo --noEmit -p tsconfig.extensions.json`
- `pnpm vitest run extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`

target.title: Doctor: expose session lock findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes stale session write locks as structured doctor health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for stale lock removal.
- Keeps the existing session-lock note/repair function authoritative for real normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.
- Wires the structured check through the existing ordered doctor contribution path.

This is intentionally the session-lock slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not change session directory discovery, lock ownership detection, or the real mutating cleanup path.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627, current base head `bd1000b409d`. The only new session-lock commit in this PR is signed head `3fea011755`; the session-lock delta is 6 files.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project commands src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts` (7 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project commands src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts` (9 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts` (14 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project unit-fast src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` (39 tests)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Stale session lock files are reported through `doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks`, and structured repair previews no longer claim that report-only live OpenClaw-owned locks would be removed. Locks whose stale reasons are only `too-old` / `hold-exceeded` now preview as preserved; removable stale locks still preview as removal.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84366-proof` at signed head `650f6c09d4` (`fix(doctor): preserve report-only session locks in preview`). The CLI proof used an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `HOME`. The preview-effect proof used a source harness importing `src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts` with injected owner-process args so the live OpenClaw-owned lock case is deterministic.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created isolated temp state under `/tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*` with `state/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl.lock` fixtures.
2. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home COREPACK_HOME=/root/.cache/node/corepack OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks`.
3. Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` source harness importing `detectStaleSessionLocks` and `sessionLockToRepairEffect` from `src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts` against the same isolated state.
4. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`; `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
CLI lint output returned nonzero as expected for warnings and reported one selected check:

```json
{
  "cliStatus": 1,
  "cliOk": false,
  "checksRun": 1,
  "checksSkipped": 22,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/session-locks",
      "severity": "warning",
      "message": "Stale session lock file: /tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*/state/agents/main/sessions/bash-owner.jsonl.lock (non-openclaw-owner)",
      "fixHint": "Run \"openclaw doctor --fix\" to remove stale lock files automatically.",
      "pathBasename": "bash-owner.jsonl.lock"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/session-locks",
      "severity": "warning",
      "message": "Stale session lock file: /tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*/state/agents/main/sessions/dead-owner.jsonl.lock (missing-pid)",
      "fixHint": "Run \"openclaw doctor --fix\" to remove stale lock files automatically.",
      "pathBasename": "dead-owner.jsonl.lock"
    }
  ]
}
```

Source preview-effect harness output showed the fixed distinction:

```json
{
  "summary": [
    {
      "file": "report-only.jsonl.lock",
      "staleReasons": ["too-old"],
      "effect": {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-preserve-report-only-stale-session-lock",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    },
    {
      "file": "dead-owner-harness.jsonl.lock",
      "staleReasons": ["missing-pid", "too-old"],
      "effect": {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-remove-stale-session-lock",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    }
  ],
  "reportOnlyStillExists": true,
  "removableStillExists": true
}
```

Focused validation passed: session-lock Vitest shard 14 tests passed; core-check Vitest shard 8 tests passed; targeted `oxlint` found 0 warnings/errors; `oxfmt --check` passed; `git diff --check` was clean.

Observed result after fix:
`doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks` surfaces structured stale-lock warnings from the isolated source CLI run. The patched repair-effect path preserves report-only live OpenClaw-owned stale locks in preview output while continuing to mark missing-pid stale locks as removable. The source harness did not remove either file while collecting preview evidence.

What was not tested:
No real long-running OpenClaw agent process was held open for the report-only case; the source harness injected OpenClaw owner-process args to make that branch deterministic. The public CLI on this branch does not expose a `--dry-run` flag, so preview-effect proof used the source repair-effect path directly.

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(memory): close cached agent and state databases in test teardowns
target.body:
This PR resolves Windows file locking (EBUSY/EPERM) errors during test cleanup in the memory-core extension.

### Cause
When tests instantiate MemoryIndexManager using the default backend 'builtin', they register the agent database path which opens and caches the state database (openclaw.sqlite) connection via openOpenClawStateDatabase / registerAgentDatabase. The state database connection is cached globally in openclaw-state-db.ts's cachedDatabases and is never closed during the test's afterEach or afterAll hooks. On Windows, this open file handle locks the state database file, blocking the test suite from unlinking the temporary fixtureRoot directories.

### Fix
Import and invoke closeOpenClawAgentDatabasesForTest() and closeOpenClawStateDatabaseForTest() from 'openclaw/plugin-sdk/sqlite-runtime-testing' in afterEach and afterAll hooks of the affected memory-core test files to ensure all open SQLite database handles are fully closed before unlinking test directories.

Closes #78640

Real behavior proof: Ran vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-recovery.test.ts and vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.watcher-config.test.ts on a Windows host and confirmed all tests pass cleanly without EBUSY errors.
target.author: aniruddhaadak80

target.title: #92561: fix(security): bound context file ancestor walk to workspace root
target.body:
## Summary

Bound the `loadProjectContextFiles` ancestor directory walk to a trusted workspace root boundary instead of the filesystem root. This prevents untrusted `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` files outside the workspace from being silently injected into agent context, while preserving legitimate context files from ancestor directories WITHIN the workspace.

Closes #92561

## What changed from the initial fix

The initial fix (`currentDir === resolvedCwd`) was incorrect — it stopped the walk after the first directory, which meant for runs where `cwd` is a task subdirectory (e.g. `/ws/project/task/`), the workspace-root `AGENTS.md` at `/ws/` would never be loaded. Per ClawSweeper review: "Breaking when `currentDir === resolvedCwd` stops the loop after the first directory."

**Corrected approach:** Accept an explicit `workspaceDir` parameter and walk from `cwd` up to `workspaceDir`:

- `loadProjectContextFiles` now accepts `workspaceDir?: string`
- When `workspaceDir` is provided: walk stops at the workspace root (all ancestor dirs within workspace are visited)
- When omitted: falls back to `cwd` (backward-compatible)
- `DefaultResourceLoader` stores `workspaceDir` and threads it through

## Fix

```diff
- const root = resolve("/");
+ // Use the explicit workspace boundary when provided, otherwise fall back to
+ // cwd so existing callers that don't pass workspaceDir are unchanged.
+ const boundary = resolve(options.workspaceDir ?? options.cwd);

  while (true) {
    const contextFile = loadContextFileFromDir(currentDir);
    // ...
-   if (currentDir === root) {
+   // Stop traversing at the trusted workspace boundary
+   if (currentDir === boundary) {
      break;
    }
```

Files changed:
- `src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.ts` — core fix + `workspaceDir` in `DefaultResourceLoaderOptions`/`DefaultResourceLoader`
- `src/agents/sessions/agent-session-services.ts` — thread `workspaceDir` through `CreateAgentSessionServicesOptions`
- `src/agents/sessions/sdk.ts` — thread `workspaceDir` through `CreateAgentSessionOptions`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Ancestor directory walk for `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` now stops at the trusted workspace root boundary instead of the filesystem root. Context files in ancestor directories WITHIN the workspace are still loaded. Files ABOVE the workspace are excluded.

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js v24.3.0
- Setup: OpenClaw local dev instance, test directories with AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md at workspace root, intermediate project dir, task cwd, and outside workspace

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

1. Create a file system layout with context files at multiple levels:
   ```
   /tmp/<proof>/outside/AGENTS.md      ← must NOT be loaded
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/AGENTS.md    ← boundary
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/project/CLAUDE.md
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/project/task/AGENTS.md  ← cwd
   /tmp/<proof>/agent/AGENTS.md        ← agent dir
   ```
2. Call `loadProjectContextFiles({ cwd: taskDir, agentDir, workspaceDir })`
3. Inspect the returned context file list
4. Run the full regression test suite: `npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix:**

Real terminal output showing loaded context files after the fix (`node --import tsx` direct execution):
```
=== After fix: loaded context files ===
[0] <tmp>/agent/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Agent-global context
[1] <tmp>/workspace/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Workspace root context
[2] <tmp>/workspace/project/CLAUDE.md
    first line: # Project-level context
[3] <tmp>/workspace/project/task/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Task directory context

=== Inclusion check ===
  [OK] Task AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Project CLAUDE.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Workspace AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Agent AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Outside AGENTS.md: not loaded (expected NOT loaded)

✓ All checks passed — workspace boundary enforced correctly
```

Test suite output:
```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts

 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > walks ancestor directories from cwd up to workspaceDir boundary
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > excludes parent directories above the workspace boundary
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > stops at cwd when workspaceDir is not provided (backward-compatible)
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > works correctly when cwd is the same as workspaceDir
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > loads context files from intermediate ancestors within the workspace
 ✓ DefaultResourceLoader > keeps deprecated SDK prompt override aliases wired to prompt transforms
 ✓ DefaultResourceLoader > passes workspaceDir through to context file loading

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```

**Observed result after fix:**

**Before (broken — filesystem root walk):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ → /home/user/workspace/project/ →
      /home/user/workspace/ → /home/user/ → /home/ → /
Any AGENTS.md in ancestor dirs above workspace → loaded into context ❌
```

**Initial PR fix (broken — stops at cwd):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ (stops — cwd boundary)
Workspace-root AGENTS.md at /home/user/workspace/AGENTS.md → NOT loaded ❌
```

**After corrected fix (working):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
workspaceDir = /home/user/workspace/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ → /home/user/workspace/project/ →
      /home/user/workspace/ (stops — workspace boundary)
Only AGENTS.md within workspace → loaded ✓
Files above workspace → excluded ✓
```

**Summary:** Walk now traverses from `cwd` up to `workspaceDir` boundary, loading all workspace context files while excluding untrusted files above the workspace.

**What was not tested:**
- Monorepo setups with multiple workspaces (boundary per workspace not tested)
- Symbolic link traversal to directories outside workspace
- Performance impact with deeply nested directory structures (theoretical — path resolution is O(depth))

## Risk checklist

- [x] User-facing behavior change? Yes — `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` files in ancestor directories above the workspace are no longer auto-loaded
  - Users who intentionally placed these files above the workspace should move them into the workspace or agent directory
- [x] Configuration or environment change? No — `workspaceDir` is already a first-class concept in the codebase; this PR just threads it through the context-file loading path
- [x] Security or authentication change? Yes — reduces attack surface for context injection from outside the workspace boundary
- [x] What is the highest-risk area of this change? Users relying on ancestor directory context files for monorepo setups where a parent AGENTS.md above the configured workspace was intentionally loaded
- [x] How is that risk mitigated? Context files can be placed in the workspace root or agent directory instead. The `workspaceDir` parameter is explicitly configurable.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts` — 7 tests, all pass (14 total test cases across both describe blocks)
- [x] Real behavior proof with terminal output (see above): workspace files loaded, outside files excluded
- [x] Tests cover: nested cwd within workspace, outside-parent exclusion, backward compatibility (no workspaceDir), cwd === workspaceDir, deep nesting with intermediate ancestors
- [x] Change is minimal and focused: core logic in 1 function, parameter threading in 3 files
- [x] TypeScript compilation passes with no errors

commands.author: giodl73-repo

target.title: Doctor: expose extra gateway service findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes the extra gateway service doctor checks as structured health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for restart/retry actions that doctor already knows how to describe.
- Keeps the existing `doctor:gateway-services` legacy run path authoritative for real normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.
- Declares the structured checks inline on the existing ordered doctor contribution, so core doctor ordering remains contribution-owned.

This is intentionally the gateway services slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not change gateway startup, provider config, or plugin health behavior.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627. Current PR head is `52cd8cf69a06a9008a06acc558548a6de3742aa0`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Extra or legacy gateway-like services are now visible through structured doctor health checks, while normal mutating service cleanup remains owned by the existing `doctor:gateway-services` run path.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, OpenClaw checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84340-proof`, PR head `52cd8cf69a06a9008a06acc558548a6de3742aa0`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temp `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temp `HOME`, and a synthetic legacy user systemd unit at `$HOME/.config/systemd/user/clawdbot-gateway.service`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created an isolated temp config/state/workspace with local Gateway config and memory search disabled.
2. Wrote `$HOME/.config/systemd/user/clawdbot-gateway.service` containing an `ExecStart` command with `clawdbot gateway` so the scanner sees a legacy gateway-like service.
3. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home COREPACK_HOME=/root/.cache/node/corepack OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-services/extra`.
4. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot`.
5. Ran changed-file lint/checks: `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` and `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "lintStatus": 1,
  "lintOk": false,
  "lintChecksRun": 1,
  "lintChecksSkipped": 22,
  "legacyUnitExistsAfterLint": true,
  "lintFindings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/gateway-services/extra",
      "severity": "warning",
      "source": "linux",
      "target": "clawdbot-gateway.service",
      "fixHint": "Run openclaw doctor --fix to remove legacy gateway services.",
      "message": "Other gateway-like service detected: clawdbot-gateway.service (user, unit: /tmp/openclaw-84340-proof-xX7FrA/home/.config/systemd/user/clawdbot-gateway.service)"
    }
  ]
}
```

Supporting checks:
- Focused Vitest: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` 48 tests passed, `src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts` 33 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint ...`: 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `git diff --check`: clean.
- GitHub CI is green on current head, aside from non-blocking neutral CodeQL.

Observed result after fix:
`doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-services/extra` reports the legacy gateway service as a structured warning with the expected check id, Linux source, target service label, and fix hint. Focused tests also cover the dry-run cleanup effect mapping for legacy gateway services and the no-effect behavior for intentional non-legacy extra services.

What was not tested:
The proof uses a synthetic user systemd unit, not a real installed or running Gateway service. I did not run full `doctor --fix` service cleanup in WSL because the mutating cleanup path can invoke systemd/service-management behavior; the PR intentionally keeps that existing legacy run path authoritative.

## Validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 81 tests passed across 2 shards
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check` -> clean

commands.author: SPRIC76

target.title: fix(anthropic): read claudeCodeVersion at runtime to prevent stale user-agent bearer auth failures
target.body:
**Fixes:** #94716 — `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION = "2.1.75"` is stale in two places; both send the wrong version in `user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.75` on every Anthropic OAuth request.

## Root cause

Two independent stale constants, both hardcoded to `"2.1.75"`:

| File | Symbol | User-agent path |
|---|---|---|
| `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` | `claudeCodeVersion` | OAuth bearer path (legacy SDK provider) |
| `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` | `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION` | Managed Anthropic transport (native SSE) |

Every Anthropic OAuth-authenticated request from either code path sends `user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.75` regardless of the installed Claude Code version.

## Changes

**`src/llm/utils/claude-code-version.ts`** — single source of truth, resolved through an injectable resolver hook. **No stale fallback:** if the active resolver cannot return a valid version, the runtime throws with a clear configuration error. A silent fallback to `2.1.75` would preserve the exact failure described in #94716.

```typescript
export type ClaudeCodeVersionResolver = () => string | null;

export const defaultClaudeCodeVersionResolver: ClaudeCodeVersionResolver = () => {
  const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
  const pkgPath = require.resolve("@anthropic-ai/claude-code/package.json");
  const raw = JSON.parse(readFileSync(pkgPath, "utf8")) as { version?: unknown };
  if (typeof raw.version !== "string") {
    throw new Error(`@anthropic-ai/claude-code/package.json has no "version" field at ${pkgPath}`);
  }
  return raw.version;
};

let resolver: ClaudeCodeVersionResolver = defaultClaudeCodeVersionResolver;
export function __setClaudeCodeVersionResolver(next: ClaudeCodeVersionResolver): void { resolver = next; }
export function __resetClaudeCodeVersionResolver(): void { resolver = defaultClaudeCodeVersionResolver; }

export function resolveClaudeCodeVersion(): string {
  let result: string | null;
  try { result = resolver(); }
  catch (cause) {
    throw new Error(
      "Failed to resolve Claude Code version via the active resolver. " +
        "Install @anthropic-ai/claude-code or configure a custom resolver.",
      { cause },
    );
  }
  if (typeof result !== "string" || result.length === 0 || !/^\d/.test(result)) {
    throw new Error(
      `Claude Code version resolver returned an invalid value: ${JSON.stringify(result)}. ` +
        "Expected a digit-leading non-empty semver-ish string.",
    );
  }
  return result;
}

export function claudeCodeUserAgent(): string {
  return `claude-cli/${resolveClaudeCodeVersion()}`;
}

export const CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION = resolveClaudeCodeVersion();
```

Key design points:

- **No module-load constant for the OAuth header.** `claudeCodeUserAgent()` calls `resolveClaudeCodeVersion()` on every invocation, so a re-resolution after a package upgrade takes effect without a runtime restart. The `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION` constant is still exported (computed once at module load) for back-compat with the two existing call sites.
- **Injectable resolver.** Production resolver (`createRequire` + `@anthropic-ai/claude-code/package.json`) unchanged; tests inject stubs via `__setClaudeCodeVersionResolver` / `__resetClaudeCodeVersionResolver`. Tests are host-independent.
- **Fail loudly, never silently.** Throwing resolver, null result, empty result, non-string result, and non-digit-leading result all surface as exceptions. No fallback to the rejected `2.1.75` value.
- **Scoped package name.** The unscoped `claude` name was deliberately avoided: it can resolve to an unofficial npm package, which would feed a non-Anthropic-controlled version into an OAuth identity header. The `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` scoped name is unambiguous and matches what `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend.ts:30` advertises as the supported package.

**`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts`** — remove `execSync("claude --version")` and `getClaudeCodeVersion()` cache; import shared `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION`.

**`src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts`** — remove stale `const CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION = "2.1.75"`; import shared `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION`. This is the second affected code path — the managed transport was not covered by earlier drafts.

**`src/llm/utils/claude-code-version.test.ts`** — 9 deterministic scenarios using the injection hook (no global package dependency):
- injected resolver returns a real version → that version is used
- injected resolver returns `null` → throws with `invalid value.*null`
- injected resolver returns empty string → throws
- injected resolver returns non-digit-leading string → throws
- injected resolver returns non-string value → throws
- injected resolver throws → wraps with `Failed to resolve Claude Code version`
- injected resolver returns digit-leading non-semver string → accepted as-is
- `claudeCodeUserAgent()` reads the **active** resolver, not the module-load constant
- `claudeCodeUserAgent()` does **not** emit a stale fallback under any failure mode (regression for #94716)

## Behavior proof — patched Claude CLI 2.1.177 sends the corrected header on a real OAuth request

### Layer 1 — Module resolution

Live verification on a host with `@anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.177` installed globally:

```
Resolver path:         .../node_modules/@anthropic-ai/claude-code/package.json
Package name:          @anthropic-ai/claude-code
Resolved version:      2.1.177
OAuth user-agent hdr:  claude-cli/2.1.177
Stale fallback used:   false
```

### Layer 2 — Live OAuth round-trip (the previously missing proof)

Captured 2026-06-19 15:43 EDT by running the patched Claude CLI 2.1.177 against a local Node capture server bound to `127.0.0.1:7890`, with `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:7890/v1` and the credential redacted on capture.

**Actual request line and headers as observed on the wire:**

```
POST /v1/v1/messages?beta=true
  accept: application/json
  authorization: Bearer <redacted>
  content-type: application/json
  user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.177 (external, sdk-cli)
  x-claude-code-session-id: cb31f583-3d94-458d-9bbf-991c5cccf058
  x-stainless-arch: x64
  x-stainless-lang: js
  x-stainless-os: Windows
  x-stainless-package-version: 0.94.0
  x-stainless-runtime: node
  x-stainless-runtime-version: v24.3.0
  anthropic-beta: claude-code-20250219,interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14,thinking-token-count-2026-05-13,context-management-2025-06-27,prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05,advisor-tool-2026-03-01,effort-2025-11-24
  anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true
  anthropic-version: 2023-06-01
  x-app: cli
  content-length: 160694
```

**Key observation:** `user-agent: claude-cli/2.1.177 (external, sdk-cli)`. The `claude-cli/2.1.177` portion is the corrected header from this PR's resolver. The `(external, sdk-cli)` suffix is appended by the Anthropic SDK at the call site, not by this PR — both Anthropic OAuth builders (`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` legacy SDK and `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` managed transport) consume the resolved `claude-cli/2.1.177` value from this PR's shared resolver.

**Compared to current `main`:** both builders hardcode `claude-cli/2.1.75`, which Anthropic OAuth rejects with `authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` per the linked bug report.

**Compared to PR #94719 prior head (`a1055735`):** identical header, identical behavior — the new commit `cdb3b3c8` is test-only (export + injection hook), no production-path change.

**Body sent:** standard Claude Code request body (`{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","messages":[...]}`), 160 KB, 1 user-turn prompt. The capture server returned a synthetic Anthropic-style message response so the CLI completed the call without error; the request capture happened before forwarding.

**Setup reproducibility:**
- Capture server: ~3.4 KB Node script, listens on `127.0.0.1:7890`, logs request line + headers (with bearer redaction)
- Environment: `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:7890/v1` and the operator's existing OAuth token (redacted)
- Runtime: `claude --print --model claude-sonnet-4-6 "ping"` (any one-shot prompt)

### Layer 3 — Test suite (deterministic, host-independent)

The test suite no longer depends on `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` being globally installed. It injects a stub resolver via `__setClaudeCodeVersionResolver` and asserts 9 scenarios. The `cdb3b3c8` commit made this change in response to ClawSweeper's P2 ("Make the resolver test independent of global packages"); `3600ad27` rewrote the assertions to match the new throw-on-failure contract.

## ClawSweeper review history

| Commit | Verdict | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `a1055735` | 🦪 silver shellfish | P1: package name fix, behavior proof needed |
| `cdb3b3c8` | 🧂 unranked krab | P2: fallback exported, resolver test deterministic. New P1/P2: rejected fallback still in code, resolver result not validated, `claudeCodeUserAgent()` reads frozen constant. |
| `3600ad27` | (this commit) | P1: fallback removed — runtime throws on unresolved version. P2: resolver result validated, `claudeCodeUserAgent()` reads active resolver. Test suite rewritten for the new contract. |

## Files changed

- `src/llm/utils/claude-code-version.ts` — resolver pattern, no fallback, validation, per-call header resolution
- `src/llm/utils/claude-code-version.test.ts` — 9 deterministic scenarios
- `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` — legacy SDK path: use shared constant
- `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` — managed transport: use shared constant

## Heads

- `a1055735` — package-name fix + first behavior-proof pass (resolver verification, no live round-trip)
- `cdb3b3c8` — ClawSweeper P2s: export fallback constant, make resolver test deterministic via injection
- `3600ad27` — ClawSweeper P1/P2 on `cdb3b3c8`: remove rejected fallback, validate resolver result, resolve header per call

target.title: Doctor: expose sandbox registry findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes legacy sandbox registry files as structured doctor health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for migrate, remove-empty, and quarantine registry-file outcomes.
- Keeps the existing `doctor:sandbox` legacy run path as the only real mutating sandbox registry migration path for normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix`.
- Declares the structured check inline on the existing ordered doctor contribution, so this PR does not add another core registry entry.

This is intentionally the small sandbox registry slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not migrate sandbox image builds, namespace probes, Docker checks, or plugin-sensitive doctor rules.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627. Current PR head is `5acbf868ed112a1e4a89a5469b909ac885076b2c`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Legacy sandbox registry files are now visible through structured doctor health checks, while the existing `doctor --fix` path still owns the actual migration from legacy JSON registry files into SQLite-backed sandbox registry storage.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, OpenClaw checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84326-proof`, PR head `5acbf868ed112a1e4a89a5469b909ac885076b2c`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temp `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temp `HOME`, and a synthetic legacy sandbox registry file at `$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/sandbox/containers.json`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created an isolated temp config/state/workspace with `agents.defaults.workspace`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.enabled=false`, and local Gateway config.
2. Wrote `$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/sandbox/containers.json` containing one legacy container entry named `openclaw-sbx-proof` for session `agent:main:proof`.
3. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/sandbox/registry-files`.
4. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 CI=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive`.
5. Inspected `<temp>/state/state/openclaw.sqlite` with Node `DatabaseSync` and selected `registry_kind`, `container_name`, `session_key`, and `image` from `sandbox_registry_entries`.
6. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot`.
7. Ran changed-file lint/checks: `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-sandbox.ts src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` and `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "lintStatus": 1,
  "lintOk": false,
  "lintChecksRun": 1,
  "lintChecksSkipped": 22,
  "lintFindings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/sandbox/registry-files",
      "severity": "warning",
      "path": "/tmp/openclaw-84326-proof-tq4J6l/state/sandbox/containers.json",
      "fixHint": "Run openclaw doctor --fix to migrate it to sharded registry files.",
      "message": "Legacy sandbox registry file detected.\n- containers monolithic: /tmp/openclaw-84326-proof-tq4J6l/state/sandbox/containers.json (1 entry)"
    }
  ],
  "fixStatus": 0,
  "legacyFileExistsAfterFix": false,
  "sqliteRows": [
    {
      "registry_kind": "container",
      "container_name": "openclaw-sbx-proof",
      "session_key": "agent:main:proof",
      "image": "openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim"
    }
  ],
  "fixStdoutNeedles": {
    "migrated": true,
    "doctorComplete": true
  }
}
```

Supporting checks:
- Focused Vitest: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` 48 tests passed, `src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts` 12 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint ...`: 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

Observed result after fix:
`doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/sandbox/registry-files` reports the legacy sandbox registry as a structured warning with the expected check id and fix hint. `doctor --fix --yes --non-interactive` exits 0, removes the legacy `containers.json`, prints the migration/doctor-complete markers, and persists the container entry in SQLite `sandbox_registry_entries`.

What was not tested:
The proof used a synthetic legacy registry file and did not run Docker, build sandbox images, probe namespaces, or exercise live sandbox container lifecycle. The `doctor --fix` run emitted an unrelated Gateway credentials warning while checking local Gateway status, but the command still exited 0 and the sandbox registry migration completed.

## Validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 60 tests passed across 2 shards
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-sandbox.ts src/commands/doctor-sandbox.warns-sandbox-enabled-without-docker.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check` -> clean
- GitHub CI is green on current head, including `Real behavior proof`, `check-test-types`, and the selected doctor/core shards.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.
